text:
Time is the strangest of all things we claim to understand. We measure it, divide it, sell it, and mourn its passing, yet no one has ever held a moment in their hands or pointed to the place where the present becomes the past.

There is something quietly astonishing about the fact that every experience we have ever had is gone in the instant we register it. Memory gives us a story about the past, and anticipation gives us a sketch of the future, but we only ever live in this thin, almost fictional sliver called now. And even as you read that word, it has already slipped away.

What strikes me most is how differently time moves depending on what we are doing with it. An hour of waiting can feel like a week. A year of joy can feel, in retrospect, like an afternoon. This suggests that our sense of time is less about clocks and more about attention. When we are fully absorbed in something, time seems to vanish. When we are counting the minutes, they refuse to move. In some sense, then, the richness of a life is not measured in years but in the depth with which those years were inhabited.

There is also something worth sitting with in the irreversibility of it all. Every moment is a door that closes behind you. You can revisit a place, reread a book, call an old friend, but you cannot go back to the specific living texture of a day that has passed. This could be cause for grief, and sometimes it is. But it is also what gives any moment its weight. The fact that this afternoon will never come again is precisely what makes it matter.

Time, finally, is not something that passes. We pass through it, or perhaps with it, carried along in a current we did not choose and cannot stop, toward something we cannot see.
